Variable MOH Budget = Variable OH rate × Budgeted DLH. Rate = $3/DLH, Budgeted DLH = 5,000. Variable MOH Budget =
2-11.e: Variable MOH = $3/DLH × 5,000 DLH = $15,000.

2-11.q: Total MOH Budget = Variable OH + Fixed OH = $15,000 + $20,000 =
2-11.e: Total Budgeted MOH = Variable OH ($15,000) + Fixed OH ($20,000) = $35,000. Both fixed and variable components must be included in the manufacturing overhead budget.

2-11.q: The COGS in the budgeted income statement includes:
o: DM + DL + Manufacturing Overhead (all product costs)
2-11.e: Budgeted COGS = DM + DL + MOH = ALL MANUFACTURING PRODUCT COSTS. Selling & Administrative expenses are NOT in COGS — they appear as a separate period cost expense below gross profit.

2-11.q: Gross Profit = Budgeted Sales - Budgeted COGS. If Sales = $500,000 and COGS = $320,000:
2-11.e: Gross Profit = $500,000 - $320,000 = $180,000. Gross profit measures the profitability of production and sales before S&A expenses.

2-11.q: The DL budget uses PRODUCTION units (not sales units) because:
o: Sales units are unknown at this stage
DL is incurred during PRODUCTION, not when goods are sold
Sales and production volumes are always equal
2-11.e: DL is incurred in the FACTORY during PRODUCTION — not when goods are sold. Therefore, the DL budget is based on PRODUCTION UNITS (which may differ from sales units due to inventory changes). Same logic applies to DM and MOH budgets.

2-11.q: When preparing the DM budget, the amount purchased differs from usage because of:
o: Changes in RAW MATERIALS INVENTORY (beginning and desired ending RM)
2-11.e: DM Purchases = DM Usage ± RM Inventory changes. If you want MORE ending RM inventory, buy more than you use. If beginning RM is higher than ending desired, buy less. The inventory bridge is the key difference between USAGE and PURCHASES.

2-11.q: S&A expenses appear in the budgeted income statement:
o: Below Gross Profit as operating expenses
2-11.e: S&A expenses are PERIOD COSTS — expensed when incurred, appearing BELOW GROSS PROFIT as operating expenses. Income Statement: Sales - COGS = Gross Profit - S&A = Operating Income. S&A is NEVER in COGS or inventory.

2-12.q: The cash budget is PRIMARILY used to:
o: Ensure sufficient liquidity and identify when borrowing is needed
2-12.e: Cash budget tracks CASH INFLOWS and OUTFLOWS to ensure the company has SUFFICIENT LIQUIDITY. It identifies periods when cash will be SHORT (need to borrow) or EXCESS (opportunity to repay debt or invest).

2-12.q: Depreciation expense is EXCLUDED from cash payments in the cash budget because:
o: It is included in the income statement
It is not a cash outflow — no cash is paid
2-12.e: Depreciation is a NON-CASH expense — it reduces income but involves NO CASH PAYMENT. The cash was paid when the asset was purchased (reflected in the capital budget). Cash budget includes only actual cash flows.

2-12.q: January sales = $80,000. Collection pattern: 70% in month of sale, 25% next month. Cash collected in January from January sales =
2-12.e: Cash collected in January = $80,000 × 70% = $56,000. The remaining 25% ($20,000) will be collected in February.

2-12.q: A capital expenditure budget covers:
2-12.e: Capital expenditure budget = plans for LONG-TERM ASSET purchases (property, plant & equipment). Items are CAPITALIZED and DEPRECIATED over time — NOT expensed immediately. Requires separate analysis and senior management approval.

2-12.q: The budgeted balance sheet uses:
o: Beginning balances plus ALL budgeted transactions for the period
2-12.e: Budgeted balance sheet: START with BEGINNING BALANCES + add all budgeted transactions (purchases, payments, revenues, expenses, depreciation) to project the ENDING BALANCE SHEET at period end.

2-12.q: Cash budget: beginning cash $5,000. Collections $80,000. Payments $70,000. Minimum cash balance $10,000. Required borrowing =
2-12.e: Ending cash before financing = Beginning cash + Collections − Payments = $5,000 + $80,000 − $70,000 = $15,000. Since ending cash ($15,000) exceeds the minimum required ($10,000), NO BORROWING is needed. Required borrowing = $0. Borrowing would only be needed if ending cash fell BELOW the minimum balance.

2-12.q: Collections from customers in the cash budget are based on:
o: Collection patterns applied to credit sales
2-12.e: Cash collections = apply COLLECTION PATTERNS to credit sales. Example: 60% collected in sale month, 35% next month. This reflects when cash actually ARRIVES, not when revenue is EARNED (accrual accounting).

2-12.q: Capital expenditure items are CAPITALIZED meaning they are:
o: Expensed immediately in the period of purchase
Added to the balance sheet as assets and depreciated over their useful life
2-12.e: CAPITALIZE = record as an ASSET on the balance sheet and DEPRECIATE over useful life. Contrast with EXPENSE = charge to income statement immediately. Capitalization spreads the cost over the periods benefited.

2-12.q: The cash budget comes AFTER which of the following budgets?
o: Sales, production, DM, DL, OH, S&A, and capital expenditure budgets
2-12.e: The cash budget is prepared LAST among operating and financial budgets — it incorporates ALL cash transactions from: DM purchases, DL payments, OH payments, S&A payments, capital expenditures, and customer collections.

2-12.q: October sales $100,000 (50% cash, 50% credit). Credit collections: 70% in month of sale, 30% next month. Total cash collected in October =
2-12.e: Cash sales: $100,000 × 50% = $50,000 collected in October. Credit sales: $100,000 × 50% = $50,000 credit. Collections in October: $50,000 × 70% = $35,000. Total = $50,000 + $35,000 = $85,000.

2-12.q: Cash DISBURSEMENTS in the cash budget include:
o: DM payments, DL payments, MOH cash payments, S&A cash payments, debt repayments
2-12.e: Cash disbursements = ACTUAL CASH PAID: DM payments (based on purchase payment terms), DL (usually paid when incurred), variable OH cash items, S&A cash expenses, capital expenditure payments, and debt repayments. NON-CASH items (depreciation) are EXCLUDED.

2-12.q: The minimum cash balance is maintained to:
o: Provide a SAFETY BUFFER for unexpected cash needs and satisfy lender requirements
2-12.e: Minimum cash balance = SAFETY BUFFER against unexpected cash outflows plus any COMPENSATING BALANCE required by lenders (banks often require minimum balances as a loan condition). Without a minimum, the company risks running out of cash.

2-12.q: Beginning cash $8,000. Receipts $90,000. Payments $85,000. Ending cash =
2-12.e: Ending Cash = Beginning + Receipts - Disbursements = $8,000 + $90,000 - $85,000 = $13,000.

2-12.q: If ending cash ($13,000) exceeds the minimum required balance ($10,000) by $3,000, the company should:
o: Repay $3,000 of existing debt or invest the surplus
2-12.e: Ending cash ($13,000) > minimum ($10,000) → $3,000 SURPLUS. The company can use the surplus to REPAY existing short-term borrowings (reducing interest cost) or invest in short-term securities. No borrowing is needed.

2-12.q: In the cash budget, DM payments are based on:
o: When DM is USED in production
When DM PURCHASES are PAID (applying payment terms to purchases)
2-12.e: Cash payments for DM = apply PAYMENT TERMS to DM PURCHASES. Example: if terms are 70% paid in purchase month and 30% next month, apply these percentages to the purchases budget. This reflects when CASH actually leaves — which may differ from when materials are received or used.

2-12.q: Which of the following is a CASH inflow in the cash budget?
2-12.e: Customer COLLECTIONS from credit sales = CASH INFLOW. Depreciation and amortization are NON-CASH expenses (no cash in or out). An accounts payable INCREASE means we received goods but haven't paid yet — no cash outflow yet.

2-12.q: Short-term borrowing in the cash budget is done when:
o: Cash balance exceeds the maximum desired
ENDING CASH would fall BELOW the minimum required balance without borrowing
2-12.e: Borrow when: Expected Ending Cash (before financing) < Minimum Cash Balance. The borrowing amount = the shortfall needed to maintain the minimum. Example: Ending cash $4,000, minimum $8,000 → borrow $4,000.

2-12.q: The budgeted balance sheet is prepared:
o: LAST — after all other budgets are complete
2-12.e: Budgeted balance sheet is prepared LAST in the budget process — it summarizes the expected financial position at period end after ALL budgeted transactions. It requires data from the income statement (retained earnings), cash budget (cash), capital budget (assets), and other budgets.

2-12.q: DM purchases paid: 80% in month of purchase, 20% next month. January purchases = $40,000. February purchases = $50,000. Cash paid in February for DM =
2-12.e: February DM cash payment = (20% of January purchases) + (80% of February purchases) = (0.20 × $40,000) + (0.80 × $50,000) = $8,000 + $40,000 = $48,000.

2-12.q: Accrual accounting DIFFERS from cash budgeting in that accrual:
o: Records revenues when EARNED and expenses when INCURRED regardless of cash timing
Is the same as cash basis
Is only used for tax purposes
2-12.e: ACCRUAL accounting: revenues recognized when EARNED, expenses when INCURRED — regardless of cash timing. CASH budget: records only when cash is RECEIVED or PAID. This creates differences — depreciation appears in accrual but NOT in cash budget; customer collections may lag sales.

2-13.q: Management by exception means managers:
o: Focus attention on significant variances that exceed a threshold
2-13.e: Management by exception: focus on SIGNIFICANT VARIANCES (above a materiality threshold). Immaterial variances are ignored. This uses management time EFFICIENTLY — investigating only where something meaningful has gone wrong.

2-13.q: A Sales Volume Variance measures:
o: The effect of selling price changes
The effect of selling more or fewer units than budgeted
The flexible budget vs actual cost difference
2-13.e: Sales Volume Variance = (Actual Units - Budgeted Units) × Budgeted CM per unit. It isolates the VOLUME effect — how much profit changed because we sold a different quantity than planned.

2-13.q: In responsibility reporting, a production manager should be evaluated on:
o: All company costs including allocated corporate overhead
Only costs they directly CONTROL — DM, DL, variable OH
Fixed rent and depreciation from headquarters
2-13.e: Responsibility reporting: managers evaluated ONLY on CONTROLLABLE costs — those they can directly influence. A production manager controls DM, DL, and variable overhead — NOT fixed rent or corporate allocated costs they cannot change.

2-13.q: Actual sales = 9,500 units. Budgeted sales = 10,000 units. Budgeted CM per unit = $20. Sales Volume Variance =
2-13.e: Sales Volume Variance = (9,500 - 10,000) × $20 = -500 × $20 = -$10,000 = $10,000 UNFAVORABLE (sold fewer units than budgeted).

2-13.q: A favorable cost variance in ongoing reporting means:
o: Actual costs were LESS than budget
2-13.e: Favorable cost variance = ACTUAL COSTS < BUDGET. The company spent LESS than planned. However, it should still be investigated — it may indicate quality reduction, production cut, or accounting error.

2-13.q: Year-to-date (cumulative) comparison in budgetary reporting:
o: Compares total actual to total budget from start of year to current date
2-13.e: YTD comparison tracks performance from the START OF THE YEAR to current date. Useful for identifying trends and seasonal patterns that monthly reporting might miss. A bad month might be offset by a good prior period in the YTD view.

2-13.q: The flexible budget variance measures:
o: Cost efficiency — was cost per unit higher or lower than standard?
The sales price vs budget difference
2-13.e: Flexible budget variance = Actual Cost - Flexible Budget Cost (same volume). It isolates EFFICIENCY — were costs higher or lower per unit than standard? Removes the volume effect, showing pure cost efficiency.

2-13.q: Actual price = $48. Budgeted price = $50. Actual units = 8,000. Sales Price Variance =
2-13.e: Sales Price Variance = (Actual Price - Budgeted Price) × Actual Units = ($48 - $50) × 8,000 = -$2 × 8,000 = -$16,000 = $16,000 UNFAVORABLE (sold at lower price than budgeted).

2-13.q: Budget reporting frequency is TYPICALLY:
2-13.e: Budget vs actual reporting is typically done MONTHLY OR QUARTERLY — frequent enough to identify problems early and take corrective action before they become major issues, but not so frequent as to create administrative burden.

2-13.q: The PRIMARY purpose of comparing actual results to budget is:
o: Performance evaluation — identifying variances for corrective action
2-13.e: The primary purpose is PERFORMANCE EVALUATION — identifying where actual results differ from plan (variances), investigating causes, and taking CORRECTIVE ACTION to get back on track or improve future planning.

2-13.q: A controllable cost is one that:
o: The manager CAN DIRECTLY INFLUENCE within their authority
2-13.e: CONTROLLABLE cost: a manager can DIRECTLY INFLUENCE the amount within their scope of authority and time frame. Controllable costs are included in the manager's responsibility report. Non-controllable (allocated, corporate) costs should be EXCLUDED from performance evaluation.

2-13.q: Responsibility accounting assigns costs to managers based on:
o: Where costs are INCURRED and WHO controls them
2-13.e: RESPONSIBILITY ACCOUNTING: assign costs to the manager who CONTROLS or INFLUENCES them. Each manager is accountable for costs under their control. This promotes accountability and motivates managers to manage costs they can actually affect.

2-13.q: A Revenue Center manager is evaluated primarily on:
2-13.e: REVENUE CENTER: responsible for generating REVENUES. Manager evaluated on sales volume, revenue targets, and sometimes selling costs. NOT responsible for production costs or asset utilization (those belong to cost centers and investment centers).

2-13.q: A Cost Center manager is evaluated on:
o: COST EFFICIENCY — actual vs budgeted costs for actual output
2-13.e: COST CENTER: responsible only for COSTS — no revenue authority. Manager evaluated on whether costs are in line with budget for the actual level of output. Examples: manufacturing departments, support functions like HR or IT.

2-13.q: A Profit Center manager is responsible for:
o: BOTH revenues AND costs (but not capital investment)
2-13.e: PROFIT CENTER: manager controls BOTH REVENUES AND COSTS — responsible for the resulting PROFIT. Evaluated on profit margins and budget variances. Examples: a product line manager, a regional division head (without capital authority).

2-13.q: Return on Investment (ROI) = Operating Income / Invested Assets. ROI is MOST appropriate for evaluating a:
2-13.e: ROI is the primary metric for INVESTMENT CENTERS — managers who control revenues, costs, AND CAPITAL INVESTMENT. ROI measures how effectively the manager uses assets to generate profit. Cost and revenue centers don't have asset authority.

2-13.q: Variance investigation: a $1,200 unfavorable DM variance on standard cost of $10,000. Should it be investigated?
o: Not necessarily — the 12% variance threshold depends on company policy
No — it is an absolute amount only
Yes — all DM variances require investigation
2-13.e: Investigation decisions depend on both ABSOLUTE AMOUNT ($1,200) and PERCENTAGE (12% of $10,000 standard). Many companies investigate variances exceeding 5%–10% of standard. A 12% variance would likely trigger investigation under most policies — but it depends on the company's specific threshold.

2-13.q: The flexible budget for the REPORTING period is prepared at:
2-13.e: The FLEXIBLE BUDGET for performance reporting is prepared at the ACTUAL ACTIVITY LEVEL achieved. This allows fair comparison: actual costs at 9,500 units vs. what SHOULD have been spent at 9,500 units. Comparing actual to the static budget at 10,000 units would be unfair.

2-13.q: A Sales Price Variance in ongoing reporting measures:
o: ACTUAL selling price vs budgeted price × actual units sold
2-13.e: Sales Price Variance = (Actual Price - Budgeted Price) × Actual Units Sold. It isolates the PRICE EFFECT — how much revenue changed because we charged a different price than planned, independent of volume changes.

2-13.q: Management by exception improves management EFFICIENCY by:
o: Requiring managers to review ONLY significant variances — not every line item
Increasing the number of budget reports
2-13.e: Management by exception: managers focus attention only on SIGNIFICANT VARIANCES (above materiality thresholds). This prevents information overload — allowing managers to spend time where it matters most, rather than investigating immaterial differences.

2-13.q: Residual Income (RI) = Operating Income − (Required Rate × Invested Assets). Division income = $200,000. Required rate = 10%. Invested assets = $1,500,000. RI =
2-13.e: RI = $200,000 − (10% × $1,500,000) = $200,000 − $150,000 = $50,000. Positive RI = division is creating value ABOVE the minimum required return. If RI = 0, division earns exactly the required return.

2-13.q: ADVANTAGE of Residual Income over ROI is that RI:
o: Avoids the goal congruence problem — managers accept any project where the incremental RI is positive
Does not require knowledge of invested assets
2-13.e: ROI Goal Congruence Problem: a division with 20% ROI will REJECT a 15% project (dilutes average). With RI, if the 15% project generates positive RI (above the 10% cost of capital), the manager SHOULD accept it — and RI correctly motivates this decision.

2-13.q: A division has ROI of 18%. A new project offers a 14% return. Cost of capital = 10%. Under ROI, the manager will likely:
o: Accept — any positive return is good
REJECT — 14% would dilute the division's 18% average ROI
2-13.e: ROI GOAL CONGRUENCE FAILURE: the division manager measures performance by ROI (currently 18%). Adding a 14% project LOWERS the average. The manager REJECTS it even though the project earns 4% above cost of capital (14% − 10%) — creating value for shareholders but hurting the manager's metric.

2-13.q: Economic Value Added (EVA) is MOST closely related to:
o: RESIDUAL INCOME — but uses after-tax NOPAT and WACC-based capital charge
2-13.e: EVA is a refined version of Residual Income: EVA = NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax) − (WACC × Invested Capital). It uses economic adjustments to accounting income and the full WACC as the capital charge. Like RI, positive EVA = value creation above the minimum required return.

2-14.q: Flexible Budget: Standard VC = $4/unit. Fixed costs = $25,000. Actual units = 8,000. Flexible budget total =
Budgeted sales 5,000. Ending FG desired 800. Beginning FG 300. Units to produce =
2-14.e: Units to Produce = 5,000 + 800 - 300 = 5,500 units.

2-14.q: Standard DM price $10/lb. Actual price $9.50/lb. Actual qty purchased 2,000 lbs. DM Price Variance =
2-14.e: DM Price Variance = (Actual - Standard) × Actual Qty = ($9.50 - $10.00) × 2,000 = -$0.50 × 2,000 = -$1,000... Wait: = ($9.50 - $10) × 2,000 = $(-0.50) × 2,000 = -$1,000 = $1,000 FAVORABLE (paid less than standard).

2-14.q: Standard DL hours allowed 3,000. Actual hours 3,200. Standard rate $15/hr. DL Efficiency Variance =
2-14.e: DL Efficiency Variance = (Actual Hours - Standard Hours Allowed) × Standard Rate = (3,200 - 3,000) × $15 = 200 × $15 = $3,000 UNFAVORABLE (used more hours than standard).

2-14.q: Actual selling price $22. Budgeted price $20. Actual units 5,000. Sales Price Variance =
2-14.e: Sales Price Variance = (Actual - Budgeted) × Actual Units = ($22 - $20) × 5,000 = $2 × 5,000 = $10,000 FAVORABLE (sold at higher price than budgeted).

2-14.q: Actual units 9,000. Budgeted units 10,000. Budgeted CM per unit $25. Sales Volume Variance =
2-14.e: Sales Volume Variance = (9,000 - 10,000) × $25 = -1,000 × $25 = -$25,000 = $25,000 UNFAVORABLE (sold 1,000 fewer units than planned).

2-14.q: Standard DM = 4 lbs/unit at $5/lb. Actual production = 1,000 units. Actual DM used = 4,200 lbs. DM Quantity Variance =
2-14.e: DM Quantity Variance = (Actual Qty - Standard Qty Allowed) × Standard Price = (4,200 - 4,000) × $5 = 200 × $5 = $1,000 UNFAVORABLE (used 200 more lbs than standard).

2-14.q: Beginning FG inventory = $15,000. Budgeted production cost = $80,000. Ending FG desired = $20,000. Budgeted COGS =
2-14.e: Budgeted COGS = Beginning FG + Production Cost - Ending FG = $15,000 + $80,000 - $20,000 = $75,000.

2-14.q: Sales = $200,000. COGS = $120,000. S&A = $40,000. Operating Income =
A favorable variance always means:
o: Actual costs were lower than budget
The company performed above expectations in that area
Actual result was BETTER than standard/budget (lower cost or higher revenue)
The budget was set too low
2-14.e: FAVORABLE = actual result was BETTER than standard/budget. For COSTS: actual < budget = favorable. For REVENUES: actual > budget = favorable. It does NOT always mean costs were lower — favorable REVENUE variance means higher revenue.

2-14.q: Total DM Cost Variance = DM Price Variance + DM Quantity Variance. DM Price = $2,000 Favorable, DM Quantity = $3,000 Unfavorable. Total DM Variance =
2-14.e: Total DM Variance = Price Variance + Quantity Variance = $2,000 F + ($3,000 U) = -$1,000 = $1,000 UNFAVORABLE. Favorable reduces cost; unfavorable increases cost. Net = $2,000 - $3,000 = -$1,000 unfavorable.

2-14.q: Total DL Cost Variance = DL Rate Variance + DL Efficiency Variance. Rate = $1,500 Unfavorable, Efficiency = $900 Favorable. Total DL Variance =
2-14.e: Total DL Variance = $1,500 U + ($900 F) = $1,500 - $900 = $600 UNFAVORABLE. The rate was worse than standard but efficiency was better — net unfavorable by $600.

2-14.q: Standard VC = $6/unit. Actual VC = $6.50/unit. Actual production = 4,000 units. Variable cost efficiency variance =
2-14.e: Variable cost spending/efficiency comparison: Actual VC = $6.50 × 4,000 = $26,000. Flexible budget VC = $6 × 4,000 = $24,000. Variance = $26,000 - $24,000 = $2,000 UNFAVORABLE (spent $0.50 more per unit than standard).

2-14.q: Contribution Margin = Sales Revenue - Variable Costs. Sales = $150,000, Variable Costs = $90,000. CM =
2-14.e: Contribution Margin = $150,000 - $90,000 = $60,000. CM represents the amount available to cover fixed costs and generate profit. CM per unit = CM / Units sold.

2-14.q: Standard variable cost per unit = $8. Actual production = 5,000 units. Flexible budget variable cost =
2-14.e: Flexible Budget VC = Standard VC per unit × Actual Production = $8 × 5,000 = $40,000. The flexible budget applies the STANDARD rate to ACTUAL volume.

2-14.q: Which variance does the PRODUCTION MANAGER primarily control?
2-14.e: The PRODUCTION MANAGER controls: DL EFFICIENCY variance (supervises workers), DM QUANTITY variance (supervises material usage), and variable OH efficiency. The PURCHASING MANAGER controls DM PRICE variance. Sales managers control sales price and volume variances.

2-14.q: Actual SP = $18, Budgeted SP = $20, Actual units = 6,000. Sales Price Variance =
2-14.e: Sales Price Variance = (Actual SP - Budgeted SP) × Actual Units = ($18 - $20) × 6,000 = -$2 × 6,000 = -$12,000 = $12,000 UNFAVORABLE (sold at lower price than budgeted).

2-14.q: Actual units 11,000. Budgeted units 10,000. Budgeted CM per unit = $30. Sales Volume Variance =
2-14.e: Sales Volume Variance = (Actual - Budgeted) × Budgeted CM = (11,000 - 10,000) × $30 = 1,000 × $30 = $30,000 FAVORABLE (sold 1,000 more units than planned).

2-14.q: Standard DM allowed = 2 lbs/unit at $4/lb. Actual production = 3,000 units. Actual DM used = 6,400 lbs. DM Quantity Variance =
2-14.e: Standard DM allowed = 3,000 × 2 = 6,000 lbs. DM Quantity Variance = (Actual - Standard Allowed) × Standard Price = (6,400 - 6,000) × $4 = 400 × $4 = $1,600 UNFAVORABLE (used 400 more lbs than standard).

2-14.q: Operating Income = Gross Profit - S&A. Gross Profit = $120,000. S&A = $45,000. Operating Income =
2-14.e: Operating Income = $120,000 - $45,000 = $75,000. Gross Profit already excludes manufacturing costs (COGS). Subtracting period costs (S&A) gives OPERATING INCOME (income from operations before interest and taxes).

2-15.q: Pro forma financial statements are used for:
o: Projecting expected results for planning and decision-making
2-15.e: Pro forma statements are PROJECTED ('AS IF') financial statements — they show expected results if a planned action (merger, expansion, new product) occurs. Used for PLANNING AND DECISION-MAKING, not historical reporting.

2-15.q: Break-even point in UNITS = Fixed Costs / CM per unit. Fixed costs = $60,000. CM per unit = $15. Break-even =
2-15.e: BEP = $60,000 / $15 = 4,000 units. At 4,000 units, total contribution margin exactly covers all fixed costs, resulting in zero operating profit.

2-15.q: Scenario analysis differs from sensitivity analysis because:
o: It changes ONE variable at a time
It defines complete alternative futures (best/base/worst) with all variables changed
It only works for financial projections
2-15.e: Scenario analysis: defines COMPLETE ALTERNATIVE SCENARIOS — all variables change together in a coherent story (best case: high growth + low costs; worst case: low growth + high costs). Sensitivity: changes ONE variable at a time.

2-15.q: Fixed costs = $80,000. Actual revenue = $200,000. BEP revenue = $160,000. Margin of Safety =
2-15.e: Margin of Safety = Actual Sales - Break-Even Sales = $200,000 - $160,000 = $40,000. This is the BUFFER — how much revenue could decline before the company starts losing money.

2-15.q: Required units to achieve target profit: FC = $50,000, Target profit = $30,000, CM = $20/unit. Required units =
2-15.e: Required Units = (FC + Target Profit) / CM = ($50,000 + $30,000) / $20 = $80,000 / $20 = 4,000 units.

2-15.q: Long-range plans typically cover:
2-15.e: Long-range (strategic) plans typically cover 3–5+ YEARS. They are directional and less detailed, setting the framework within which annual (1-year) budgets are prepared. Annual budgets implement the long-range plan for the current year.

2-15.q: A what-if analysis model allows:
o: Only one scenario to be tested
Interactive modeling of multiple assumption combinations
2-15.e: What-if analysis = INTERACTIVE MODELING allowing users to change multiple assumptions and immediately see the effect on outcomes. It explores a range of possible scenarios — more flexible than formal scenario analysis.

2-15.q: The relationship between annual budget and long-range plan is:
o: Annual budget provides 1-year detail within the long-range framework
They are independent of each other
2-15.e: Annual budget = detailed 1-YEAR IMPLEMENTATION of the long-range strategic plan. The long-range plan sets strategic direction (3-5+ years); the annual budget translates this into specific targets for the current year.

2-15.q: Break-even analysis in top-level planning helps management understand:
o: The minimum activity level required to avoid losses
2-15.e: Break-even analysis shows the MINIMUM ACTIVITY LEVEL (units or revenue) needed to cover all fixed and variable costs — avoiding losses. Critical for new product decisions, pricing strategy, and assessing risk.

2-15.q: Sensitivity analysis on a new product launch shows that a 10% reduction in unit price reduces operating income by $50,000. This indicates:
o: The product is very sensitive to price — pricing decisions are critical
The fixed cost structure is too high
2-15.e: HIGH SENSITIVITY to price = a 10% price change causing a large income impact. This tells management that PRICING IS CRITICAL — getting the price right matters significantly. It should focus management attention on pricing strategy and competitive positioning.

2-15.q: Break-even point in SALES DOLLARS = Fixed Costs / Contribution Margin RATIO. Fixed Costs = $60,000. CMR = 40%. BEP Revenue =
2-15.e: BEP (Revenue) = Fixed Costs / CM Ratio = $60,000 / 0.40 = $150,000 in sales revenue needed to break even.

2-15.q: Contribution Margin Ratio (CMR) = CM / Sales. Sales = $200,000. Variable Costs = $120,000. CMR =
2-15.e: CM = $200,000 - $120,000 = $80,000. CMR = $80,000 / $200,000 = 40%. For every $1 of sales, $0.40 contributes to covering fixed costs and profit.

2-15.q: Margin of Safety % = (Actual Sales - BEP Sales) / Actual Sales. Actual = $250,000. BEP = $150,000. Margin of Safety % =
2-15.e: MOS = ($250,000 - $150,000) / $250,000 = $100,000 / $250,000 = 40%. Revenue can decline by 40% before reaching the break-even point.

2-15.q: Operating leverage measures:
o: How much debt the company uses
How SENSITIVE operating income is to CHANGES IN SALES VOLUME
The ratio of fixed to total assets
2-15.e: OPERATING LEVERAGE = sensitivity of operating income to sales volume changes. High fixed costs relative to variable costs = HIGH operating leverage. A 10% sales increase may produce 30% operating income increase (3× leverage). Both magnifies gains AND losses.

2-15.q: Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL) = CM / Operating Income. CM = $100,000. Operating Income = $25,000. DOL =
2-15.e: DOL = $100,000 / $25,000 = 4x. A 10% increase in sales will produce a 4 × 10% = 40% increase in operating income. This high leverage is due to a large fixed cost base relative to profit.

2-15.q: CVP (Cost-Volume-Profit) analysis assumes:
o: SELLING PRICE PER UNIT is constant, variable cost per unit is constant, and fixed costs remain fixed within the relevant range
The company sells multiple products with changing mix
2-15.e: CVP assumptions: (1) Selling price per unit = CONSTANT, (2) Variable cost per unit = CONSTANT, (3) Fixed costs = CONSTANT (within relevant range), (4) All costs are either purely fixed or purely variable. Mixed costs must be separated first.

2-15.q: In CVP analysis, a mixed (semi-variable) cost must be:
o: SEPARATED into fixed and variable components using High-Low or regression methods
2-15.e: Mixed costs have BOTH fixed and variable components. CVP requires all costs to be either purely fixed OR purely variable. Therefore, mixed costs must be SEPARATED (using High-Low method or regression) before incorporating them into CVP analysis.

2-15.q: Pro forma income statement shows:
o: PROJECTED revenues, costs, and profit if a planned decision is implemented
2-15.e: Pro forma income statement = PROJECTED income statement showing expected results IF a specific plan is implemented (e.g., launching new product, acquiring a company). Used in planning and decision-making, not historical reporting.

2-15.q: Segment margin analysis is used to:
o: Evaluate the PROFITABILITY OF INDIVIDUAL SEGMENTS after deducting their specific fixed costs
2-15.e: Segment margin = Revenue - Variable Costs - SEGMENT-SPECIFIC FIXED COSTS. It measures the PROFITABILITY of each segment (product, region, customer) after all costs SPECIFIC TO that segment. Excludes common/allocated fixed costs from the segment margin.

2-15.q: Break-even analysis assuming price = $50, VC = $30, FC = $60,000. How many units must be sold to earn $20,000 profit?
2-15.e: CM per unit = $50 - $30 = $20. Units needed = (FC + Target Profit) / CM = ($60,000 + $20,000) / $20 = $80,000 / $20 = 4,000 units.

2-15.q: Segment Margin = Revenue − Variable Costs − ___
2-15.e: Segment Margin = Revenue − Variable Costs − SEGMENT-SPECIFIC FIXED COSTS (those that would be AVOIDED if the segment were dropped). COMMON fixed costs (that continue regardless) are EXCLUDED from segment margin — they should not affect the drop/keep decision.

2-15.q: A product line has revenue $400,000, variable costs $250,000, segment-specific fixed costs $120,000, and allocated common fixed costs $50,000. Segment margin =
2-15.e: Segment Margin = Revenue − Variable Costs − Segment-Specific Fixed = $400,000 − $250,000 − $120,000 = $30,000. The $50,000 allocated common fixed costs are EXCLUDED — they continue regardless of this segment's existence.

2-15.q: A segment should be DROPPED only if:
o: Its SEGMENT MARGIN is NEGATIVE — meaning it cannot cover its own avoidable costs
A competitor offers the same product
2-15.e: Drop decision: drop the segment if its SEGMENT MARGIN is NEGATIVE. A negative segment margin means the segment cannot even cover the costs that would actually disappear if it were dropped. A positive segment margin — even if the segment shows an accounting loss after allocated overheads — means it is contributing to covering common costs.

2-15.q: Pro forma financial statements are PRIMARILY used for:
o: PLANNING AND DECISION-MAKING — projecting expected results if a planned action is implemented
2-15.e: Pro forma = 'as if' projected statements. Used BEFORE a decision (merger, expansion, new product launch) to project expected income statement, balance sheet, and cash flows. Not for historical reporting — purely for planning and communicating expected financial outcomes.

2-15.q: Sensitivity analysis in top-level planning is MOST useful for identifying:
o: Which KEY VARIABLES most significantly impact the plan's success or failure
2-15.e: Sensitivity analysis: change ONE variable at a time (price, volume, cost) and observe the impact. This identifies CRITICAL VARIABLES — the assumptions that matter most. Management focuses monitoring, risk management, and contingency planning on these high-sensitivity inputs.

2-16.q: NPV = $0 means a project:
o: Generates exactly the required rate of return — acceptable
2-16.e: NPV = 0 means the project generates exactly the REQUIRED RATE OF RETURN (cost of capital). The company earns exactly what investors demand. The project is ACCEPTABLE (though not creating extra value). Accept if NPV ≥ 0.

2-16.q: A company has capital rationing and must choose between 3 independent projects. The BEST method to rank them is:
o: Profitability Index — highest PI per dollar invested first
2-16.e: Under CAPITAL RATIONING, use the PROFITABILITY INDEX (PI) to rank projects. PI = PV of cash flows / Investment. It measures value created PER DOLLAR INVESTED — ensuring limited capital is allocated to the highest-return projects relative to their cost.

2-16.q: IRR of 14%, required rate of return 12%. The project should be:
o: Rejected — IRR is too close to hurdle rate
2-16.e: Accept if IRR ≥ Required Rate of Return. IRR (14%) > hurdle rate (12%) → project generates returns ABOVE what investors require → ACCEPT. The 2% excess represents additional value creation beyond the minimum required.

2-16.q: The MAIN weakness of the payback period method is that it:
o: Ignores time value of money AND ignores cash flows after the payback period
Is only useful for large projects
2-16.e: Payback has TWO key weaknesses: (1) IGNORES TIME VALUE of money — $1 in year 1 treated same as $1 in year 5, and (2) IGNORES cash flows AFTER payback — a project may recover its cost quickly but be terrible overall. These make it unsuitable as the primary capital budgeting method.

2-16.q: Initial investment $200,000. PV of future cash flows $240,000. Profitability Index =
2-16.e: PI = PV of Future Cash Flows / Initial Investment = $240,000 / $200,000 = 1.20. PI > 1.0 → accept (the project returns $1.20 for every $1.00 invested). NPV = $240,000 - $200,000 = $40,000 (also positive → accept).

2-16.q: When NPV and IRR give conflicting rankings for MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE projects, management should:
o: Always follow NPV — it directly measures value added in dollars
2-16.e: When NPV and IRR conflict on mutually exclusive projects, follow NPV. NPV directly measures DOLLAR VALUE CREATED, which is the goal of financial management. IRR's reinvestment assumption (cash flows reinvested at IRR) is often unrealistic; NPV assumes reinvestment at cost of capital, which is more realistic.

2-16.q: Which costs are IRRELEVANT in a capital budgeting decision?
o: Opportunity costs — return on best alternative
Incremental cash flows from the project
Sunk costs — already incurred and unrecoverable
2-16.e: SUNK COSTS are ALWAYS IRRELEVANT — they were incurred in the past and cannot be recovered regardless of the decision made. Only FUTURE, INCREMENTAL cash flows matter. Opportunity costs ARE relevant (benefit foregone). Salvage value IS relevant (future cash flow).

2-16.q: NPV formula: NPV = PV of future cash inflows - Initial Investment. If NPV is POSITIVE, the project:
o: Generates returns BELOW the required rate
Generates returns ABOVE the required rate — ACCEPT
2-16.e: Positive NPV means the project returns MORE than the required rate of return (cost of capital) → CREATES VALUE → ACCEPT. Negative NPV → destroys value → REJECT. Zero NPV → earns exactly the required return → marginally acceptable.

2-16.q: Sunk costs in capital budgeting are:
o: NEVER relevant — already incurred and cannot be recovered
2-16.e: SUNK COSTS = already incurred, cannot be recovered regardless of the decision made. ALWAYS IRRELEVANT to future decisions. Example: $50,000 spent on a feasibility study before deciding to invest — this cost is sunk and should not influence the investment decision.

2-16.q: Incremental cash flows in capital budgeting are:
o: Only cash flows from current operations
Cash flows that OCCUR ONLY IF the project is accepted (the CHANGE caused by the project)
2-16.e: INCREMENTAL cash flows = cash flows that happen ONLY BECAUSE the project is accepted. Only these flows are relevant to the decision. Existing cash flows that occur whether or not the project is accepted are IRRELEVANT.

2-16.q: Salvage (terminal) value in capital budgeting:
o: Is ignored in all capital budgeting methods
Is included as a CASH INFLOW at the end of the project's life
2-16.e: SALVAGE VALUE = expected proceeds from selling the asset at end of its useful life. It is a CASH INFLOW at the project's terminal year and must be included in NPV and IRR analysis. It is discounted to present value in NPV calculations.

2-16.q: The discount rate used in NPV calculations represents:
o: The company's REQUIRED RATE OF RETURN (cost of capital or hurdle rate)
2-16.e: NPV discount rate = REQUIRED RATE OF RETURN (also called cost of capital, hurdle rate, or WACC). It reflects the minimum return investors require. Using this rate, a positive NPV means the project exceeds investor requirements.

2-16.q: Payback period = Initial Investment / Annual Cash Flow. Investment = $240,000, Annual CF = $60,000. Payback =
2-16.e: Payback = $240,000 / $60,000 = 4 years. Simple and intuitive but ignores time value of money and cash flows after payback. Used as a quick liquidity and risk screening tool — not as the primary capital budgeting method.

2-16.q: Accounting Rate of Return (ARR) is based on:
o: ACCOUNTING INCOME (net income) / Average book value of investment
2-16.e: ARR = Average Annual Net Income / Average Book Value of Investment. Uses ACCOUNTING INCOME (not cash flows), therefore ignores time value of money. Not a preferred method for capital budgeting decisions but easy to calculate from financial statements.

2-16.q: Net Initial Investment in capital budgeting includes:
o: Only the purchase price of the asset
Purchase price + installation + shipping + increases in working capital - salvage of replaced asset
2-16.e: Net Initial Investment = TOTAL CASH OUTFLOW at project start: cost of new asset + installation/shipping + training + INCREASE IN NET WORKING CAPITAL - after-tax salvage value of old asset being replaced. All cash required to get the project started.

2-16.q: Depreciation is RELEVANT in capital budgeting because:
o: Depreciation creates a TAX SHIELD — reducing taxable income, which reduces cash taxes paid
It appears on the balance sheet
It is included in NPV directly
2-16.e: Depreciation itself is NOT a cash flow (irrelevant directly). BUT it creates a TAX SHIELD: Depreciation × Tax Rate = Cash Tax Savings. These tax savings ARE cash flows and MUST be included in after-tax NPV analysis. Depreciation tax shield = incremental after-tax benefit.

2-16.q: Working capital in capital budgeting: a new project requires $50,000 more in inventory and accounts receivable. This is treated as:
o: A CASH OUTFLOW (investment) at project start, recovered at project end
2-16.e: Increased working capital (higher inventory, A/R) = CASH TIED UP = a cash OUTFLOW at the start of the project. At the project's END, working capital is RELEASED (converted back to cash) = cash INFLOW. Both flows must be included in NPV analysis.

2-16.q: Independent projects are ACCEPTED or REJECTED independently. Under NPV, accept ALL projects where:
2-16.e: INDEPENDENT PROJECTS: accept ALL projects with NPV > 0 (assuming unlimited capital). Each project is evaluated on its own merit — accepting one does not preclude accepting others. Only under CAPITAL RATIONING must projects compete for limited funds.

2-16.q: Capital rationing means:
o: The company has unlimited capital to invest
The company has a FIXED CAPITAL BUDGET and must choose the best combination of projects
All projects must have positive IRR
2-16.e: CAPITAL RATIONING = limited capital budget — not enough funds to accept ALL positive-NPV projects. The company must SELECT the combination of projects that MAXIMIZES TOTAL NPV given the constraint. Profitability Index (PI) helps rank projects by value per dollar invested.

2-16.q: Mutually exclusive projects mean:
o: Only ONE project can be selected — choosing one PRECLUDES the other
2-16.e: MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE: accepting ONE project PREVENTS accepting another (e.g., two machine options to replace one old machine). Choose the ONE with the highest NPV. Independent projects can all be accepted if NPV > 0; mutually exclusive requires choosing between alternatives.

2-16.q: WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital) is used as the NPV discount rate because:
o: It represents the BLENDED RETURN REQUIRED by all capital providers (equity and debt holders) — the minimum return a project must earn
It is always lower than the IRR
It is set by the government
2-16.e: WACC = minimum return the company must earn to satisfy ALL capital providers. After-tax cost of debt (interest tax shield reduces effective rate) + cost of equity, weighted by their proportions. Using WACC ensures a project must cover the cost of ALL financing used to fund it.

2-16.q: Pre-tax cost of debt = 8%. Tax rate = 25%. After-tax cost of debt =
2-16.e: After-tax cost of debt = Pre-tax rate × (1 − Tax Rate) = 8% × (1 − 0.25) = 8% × 0.75 = 6%. Interest expense is TAX-DEDUCTIBLE — the government effectively subsidises 25% of the interest cost. This reduces the true economic cost of debt.

2-16.q: Annual depreciation = $50,000. Tax rate = 30%. Annual depreciation tax shield (cash benefit) =
2-16.e: Depreciation Tax Shield = Annual Depreciation × Tax Rate = $50,000 × 30% = $15,000. Depreciation reduces taxable income by $50,000 → reduces cash taxes paid by $15,000. This $15,000 is a REAL CASH INFLOW that must be included in after-tax NPV analysis.

2-16.q: Asset book value = $20,000. Salvage (sale) value = $50,000. Tax rate = 30%. After-tax salvage value =
2-16.e: Gain on disposal = $50,000 − $20,000 = $30,000. Tax on gain = $30,000 × 30% = $9,000. After-tax SV = $50,000 − $9,000 = $41,000. When an asset is sold above book value, the GAIN is taxable. The after-tax amount is the actual cash received net of taxes.

2-16.q: MIRR (Modified IRR) is preferred over standard IRR because:
o: MIRR ignores time value of money
MIRR ASSUMES CASH FLOWS ARE REINVESTED AT THE COST OF CAPITAL (more realistic than IRR's assumption of reinvestment at the IRR rate)
MIRR does not require a discount rate
2-16.e: Standard IRR implicitly assumes cash flows are reinvested at the IRR rate — often unrealistic (especially for high-IRR projects). MIRR explicitly assumes reinvestment at the COST OF CAPITAL, giving a more conservative and realistic return estimate. When NPV and MIRR conflict, still rely on NPV.

2-16.q: A post-audit of capital projects is performed to:
o: COMPARE actual cash flows to original projections — improving future estimates and accountability
2-16.e: Post-audit (post-completion audit): review AFTER implementation comparing actual results to the original capital budget projections. Benefits: (1) accountability — managers know projections will be verified; (2) reduces optimism bias in future proposals; (3) identifies problems early for corrective action.

2-16.q: The crossover rate on a NPV profile is the discount rate at which:
o: NPV equals zero for both projects
TWO MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE PROJECTS have EQUAL NPV — above the crossover rate, one is preferred; below it, the other is preferred
IRR equals the cost of capital
2-16.e: Crossover rate = discount rate where two mutually exclusive projects have EQUAL NPV. Below the crossover rate: one project is preferred. Above it: the other is preferred. This means NPV and IRR can give different rankings depending on which side of the crossover rate the actual WACC falls. Always use WACC as the discount rate and choose the project with higher NPV at that rate.
